coredata.dc:title: RETRACTED: Efficiency and durability of carbon dots grafted TiO2-based coatings applied on concrete under peeling and washing trials

coredata.dc:description: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (http://www.elsevier.com/locate/withdrawalpolicy). This article has been retracted at the request of the Corresponding Authors and Editor-in-Chief. One of the corresponding authors, Xiaoyi Zhang, notified the journal of several issues relating to the article and requested its retraction. These issues have been detailed below: - The experiments were not performed as reported in the article. Specifically, the coating was applied to a wood surface, rather than the reported concrete surface. When subsequent experiments were conducted using concrete, the experimental data were not reproducible. - Some of the authors informed the journal that they were not aware of the content of the manuscript, nor of their authorship. - In addition, according to Dr. Zhang: вҖңвҖҰthe pictures and data in the paper are used incorrectly, resulting in unreliable experimental resultsвҖқ. These issues represent a clear violation of our publishing policies, and publishing ethics standards. The Editor-in-Chief has assessed the case and decided to retract the article. The corresponding authors agreed to the retraction.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 Available online 7 September 2022 0925-9635/ГӮВ© 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. Efficiency and durability of carbon dots grafted TiO2-based coatings applied on concrete under peeling and washing trials Xiaoyi Zhang a,b,*, Guoliang Lin a,b, Shenglan Ma a,b, Mingyi Liu b, Wanxi Jiang c, Chen Wu a,b,* a Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology and Informatization in Civil Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, Fuzhou 350118, China b Fujian University of Technology, Fuzhou 350118, China c CNNC Huachen Engineering Management Co., Ltd, China A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Durability Carbon dots grafted TiO2-based Photocatalytic formaldehyde removal Concrete substrate A B S T R A C T In engineering practice, the durability of photocatalytic coatings is a major concern. Here two types of unique visible light-responsive coatings are proposed and placed on the concrete surface, consisting of vinyl chloride/ vinyl ester/ethylene copolymer (as a binder) and carbon-dots-grafted TiO2 (TiO2-CDs) but different in fabrica- tion. The first type is mono-layer coating (MLC), which involves directly spraying the TiO2-CDs suspension containing the binder onto the concrete. The second type was called double-layer coating (DLC), which involves applying a binder layer to the concrete surface before spraying the TiO2-CDs layer. These show that adding binder improves the coatings' anchoring on both MLC and DLC concrete substrates, along with desirable resis- tance to peeling and washing, when compared to the TiO2-CDs coated concrete without the binder. The well distributed binder in the TiO2-CDs-based coating inevitably decreases the photocatalytic efficiency of the MLC concrete due to the binder's masking effect on the coated surface. However, the DLC concrete fully exploits the binder adhesion by inserting a polymer matrix layer and therefore maintains a strong resistance to peeling and washing without compromising its photocatalytic efficiency. The proposed DLC approach enables the fabrication of highly cost-effective and durable photocatalytic coatings applied to cementitious materials. 1. Introduction Researchers' attention has shifted to the problem of indoor volatile organic pollution with the rapid expansion of the social economy. In regular usage, indoor floors, cabinets, wall panels, and other functional concrete materials, as well as textiles, wallpapers, adhesives, paints, and coatings, emit gaseous pollutants, such as formaldehyde (HCHO) and aromatics. HCHO pollution is more common among them. It is referred to as the ГўвӮ¬Е“air killerГўвӮ¬пҝҪ of the living environment because it endangers the physical and mental health of individuals. At the moment, physical adsorption, plant purification, photocatalysis, and low temperature plasma treatment are the primary methods for treating indoor air pollution. In the presence of water and oxygen, the hole-electron pairs produced by photocatalyst under solar irradiation could degrade organic [1,2] and inorganic [3,4] pollutants, as well as microorganisms [5,6]. Among many photocatalytic materials, nano-TiO2 has the advantages of being non-toxic and harmless, good chemical stability, resistance to ultraviolet light corrosion, and high cost performance, making it the most widely researched and applied green photocatalytic material [7ГўвӮ¬вҖң9]. Nano-TiO2 is used in a wide range of applications, including organic pollutant degradation, photoaging resistance, anti- bacterial deodorization, and self-cleaning coatings [10ГўвӮ¬вҖң12]. However, in practical applications, anatase nano-TiO2 is typically only efficient when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light (wavelength < 387 nm) or limited sunlight reflected in the room, and utilizing visible light re- sources is difficult. The practical application value of TiO2 photo- catalytic materials is limited [13ГўвӮ¬вҖң15]. Therefore, it is necessary to modify TiO2 to broaden its spectrum response range, resulting in a composite with an excellent visible light response and a purification function for degrading HCHO [16,17]. Quantum dot composite TiO2 refers to increasing the number of photogenerated electrons and holes in photocatalytic materials through the photosensitivity of quantum dots under ultraviolet light and visible light, and broadening their absorption * Corresponding authors at: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology and Informatization in Civil Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, Fuzhou 350118, China. E-mail addresses: xy-zhang@fjut.edu.cn (X. Zhang), wuchen2001@126.com (C. Wu). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Diamond & Related Materials journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/diamond https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.109333 Received 14 April 2022; Received in revised form 24 August 2022; Accepted 30 August 2022 RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 2 of sunlight. However, most of the traditional quantum dots contain poisonous heavy metal ions that are detrimental to human health. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have emerged as a fluorescent carbon nanomaterial. It possesses excellent optical stability, a wide source of raw materials, a wide absorption spectrum, and is non- toxic and harmless. The field of catalysis shows great potential [18ГўвӮ¬вҖң20]. It is generally believed that the introduction of CDs enhances the TiO2 system's ability to transfer photogenerated electrons and that CDs could promote the enrichment of gaseous pollutants on the surface of photo- catalytic materials to a certain extent [21ГўвӮ¬вҖң23]. Gao et al. [24] synthe- sized TiO2-CDs by mixing a hydrothermal extract of poplar wood chips with TiO2 and calcining at a high temperature. The results showed that the introduction of CDs increased the enrichment of gaseous pollutants on the surface of photocatalytic materials and improved the photo- catalytic ability of TiO2. Liang et al. [25] confirmed that nitrogen-, phosphorus-, and sulfur-doped CDs have more excellent fluorescence and photocatalytic activity, mostly due to the increased surface defects and active sites created by heteroelement doping. Martins et al. [26] used such nitrogen-doped CDs to modify TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of NOx pollutants, and the results showed that nitrogen- doped CDs may significantly improve the photocatalytic ability of the TiO2 system. Previously, photocatalytic cementitious composites intermixed with TiO2 particles for HCHO abatement have been reported [27,28]. Notably, most of photocatalyst have a difficult time participating in photo-induced reactions, leading to a high cost but low efficiency for these photocatalyst products. Photocatalytic reactions usually occur on the material's surface, which is exposed to sunlight and pollutants. Photocatalytic coatings prepared by spray coating [29,30], dip-coating [31], and electrospray coating [32] have been developed to coat the substrate surface with a uniformly dispersed photocatalyst. Although considerable progress has been made in recent years in the application of photocatalytic coatings on stone and glass [33ГўвӮ¬вҖң35], the photocatalytic mechanism of coatings on stone or glass is not necessarily applicable to cement-based materials due to the rough surface and cracks of cemen- titious materials. Research on photocatalytic coatings applied to cement-based materials is still in its infancy. Most of the reports focus on the photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalytic products, but do not consider their durability under practical use conditions [29,36]. The durability of the coating was a crucial aspect for the large-scale appli- cation of the coating. Kristof Demeestere [37] used roofing tiles con- taining TiO2 as a photocatalytic building material and found that the photocatalytic effect reached 78 ГӮВұ 2 %. Cleaning building materials with deionized water to simulate rainfall could regenerate photocatalyst activity by a factor of 1.3. Hassan [38] might be the first to evaluate the durability and resistance to wear of a TiO2 surface coating applied on concrete pavements. Chen [39] prepared optimal N-doped TiO2 and suspensions to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-con- taining mortar samples, and investigated the effects of TiO2 imple- mentation and environmental factors on the photocatalytic performance of mortar samples through NO decomposition experiments. Ramirez and colleagues [40] investigated the weathering resistance of a TiO2 coating on autoclaved aerated concrete using a dip-coating technique and found a decline in TiO2 content by >93 % after intensive weathering. Wei et al. [41] synthesized g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalysts with mesoporous structure and tight interfacial connections, and the photocatalytic activities reached 4.41 and 2.48 times that of the as-prepared g-C3N4 and meso- porous TiO2. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/TiO2 could be explained by the synergistic effect among the large specific surface area, reduced energy band gap, and tight interfacial contact. Under practical use conditions, catalysts were usually doped with various polymer matrix and fixed on substrates to prevent rain washing and abrasion, including acrylic resins [42], epoxy resins [43] and fluo- ropolymers [44]. Vinyl esters were widely used in coatings and adhe- sives due to their excellent resistance to various common conditions [45ГўвӮ¬вҖң47]. There were two different methods of making photocatalytic coatings using polymer matrix. First, the catalyst material was directly and uniformly dispersed into the binder suspension to form a composite sol suitable for spraying or brushing [48,49]. Second, an adhesive interlayer was applied to the substrate before installing the photo- catalytic layer [50,51]. The development and testing of these hybrid coatings for different substrates were mostly carried out in the labora- tory. There are only a few reports on how well these hybrid coatings applied to concrete substrates could withstand wear and rain washing [35,52]. According to a literature review, little effort has been made to motivate the polymer matrix addition technique to improve the effi- ciency and durability of photosensitive coatings on concrete surfaces. In this work, two polymer matrix addition techniques were applied to fabricate two light-responsive coatings on concrete surfaces, composed of vinyl chloride/vinyl ester/ethylene copolymer (as a binder) and TiO2- CDs. One, the binder and TiO2-CDs were mixed well to form a mixed suspension, and it was sprayed on the concrete to form a coating. The other, before coating the TiO2-CDs photocatalyst, the adhesive was sprayed on the surface of the concrete member. The TiO2-CDs photo- catalyst without adhesive was coated on the concrete member as the control group. The durability of these coated concretes was evaluated using a peeling action and simulated rain washing, as well as qualitative- quantitative SEM-EDS analysis, in terms of TiO2-CDs loss from the concrete surface. Water contact angle measurements were used to monitor the hydrophobicity of these coated concretes. The photo- catalytic performance of TiO2-CDs coatings on concrete components was evaluated by the HCHO removal ability under visible or UV light before and after durability testing (i.e., peeling test and washing test). This paper's work will provide a good reference for elevating the efficiency and durability of photocatalytic coatings on concrete member. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials and concrete substrate All chemical reagents were supplied by Aladdin-e Co., Ltd. and used without further purification. All other organic solvents and inorganic reagents were purified according to standard anhydrous methods before use. Panicum virgatum, provided by Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University was a Poaceae herb, which was used to synthesize CDs. A binder mostly composed of vinyl chloride/vinyl ester/ethylene copol- ymer (COMPAKTUNAPRO) which was obtained from P.T.B. COMPAK- TUNA. It was used to coat concrete base surfaces to increase adhesion. The characteristics of the binder was listed in Table 1. Shandong Sunnsy Group Co., Ltd. provided the 42.5 R Portsland cement (Chinese Stan- dard). The chemical composition mass percentage of cement is listed in Table 2. 3-Aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS, mass fraction 99 %) and nano-TiO2 were purchased from Aladdin-e Co., Ltd.; China ISO standard sand used in this work was obtained from Fuzhou ISO Standard Sand Co., Ltd. The concrete substrate was fabricated with commercial concrete with a design strength grade of C30. First, the dry cement was mixed with deionized water for 30 s at a low speed in a mixer. Following that, coarse and fine aggregate were added to the mixture and stirred for another 90 s (30 s at low speed and 60 s at high speed) to obtain the fresh mixtures. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Fresh concrete was cast in the specific rectangular shape (100 mm ГғвҖ” 45 mm ГғвҖ” 5 mm) and compacted on a vibration table. After, 24 h, all specimens were demolded and transferred to a curing chamber (20 ГўвҖ”ВҰC and 98 % relative humidity) for another 27 days. Lignocellulosic fluorescent CDs were synthesized by the literature [53]. Table 1 Characteristics of the binder (COMPAKTUNAГӮВ®PRO). Dynamic viscosity (mPaГўвҖ№вҖҰs) Solid content (%) pH value Density (kg/m3) 80ГўвӮ¬вҖң120 52 ГӮВұ 1 7ГўвӮ¬вҖң9 1.1 X. Zhang et al. RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 3 2.2. Synthesis of TiO2-CDs composite photocatalytic material Take 0.05 g of TiO2 nano-powder and 50 g of ethanol solution into a 100 mL conical flask, ultrasonically disperse for 15 min, and place it on a heating plate at 55 ГўвҖ”ВҰCГўвӮ¬вҖң60 ГўвҖ”ВҰC with continuous magnetic stirring. Slowly add 0.5 mL of deionized water and 0.5 mL of APTMS dropwise. After stirring for 6 h, the suspension was transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min. The centrifuged solid part was taken and washed with an ethanol solution three times to obtain an APTMS TiO2. The solid part was added to a beaker containing 100 mL of water, and then 0.5 mL of CDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg/L) was added, and the mixture was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 6ГўвӮ¬вҖң8 h to fully combine CDs with TiO2. Observe that the supernatant has no fluorescence (or weak fluorescence) under 365-nm UV light to ensure that the CDs were fully grafted to the TiO2 nanostructure surface by APTMS, denoted as TiO2-CDs. 2.3. Characterization test of material The microstructures of TiO2 and CDs nanomaterials were analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2-F30). A Scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU-8020) was used to observe the change in concrete morphology, and the distribution of composite photocatalytic nanomaterials on the concrete surface was analyzed by scanning with an EDS element energy spectrum micro-area (300 ГҺВјm ГғвҖ” 300 ГҺВјm). The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs samples were measured using a spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu Cor- poration, Japan). The phase structure changes of TiO2-CDs functionally coated were measured by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD), which was continuously scanned at a diffraction angle of 10ГўвҖ”ВҰГўвӮ¬вҖң80ГўвҖ”ВҰ at a rate of 5ГўвҖ”ВҰ/ min. On the Varian spectrometer, UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis spectra of CDs were obtained. Fluorescence spectra of CDs were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer. 2.4. Coating deposition Add 2.0-g APTMS coupling agent to 98-g absolute ethanol, and continuously magnetically stir for 1 h to promote the hydrolysis of the silane coupling agent and prepare a 2.0 % APTMS-ethanol mixed solu- tion, using the prepared solution to precoat the concrete surface. After drying at room temperature, it was transferred to a drying oven at 65 ГўвҖ”ВҰC for 2 h. Two different procedures, including single layer coating (MLC) technology and double layer coating (DLC) technology, were applied to concrete to prepare TiO2-CDs based coatings. The preparation process was shown in Fig. 1. The first step was to stir the adhesive for 1ГўвӮ¬вҖң2 h at room temperature to dissolve the adhesive uniformly in deionized water to form 1 wt% adhesive suspension. In the second step, TiO2-CDs suspensions A and B (15 g LГўЛҶвҖҷ 1) were obtained by ultrasonic treatment of TiO2-CDs powder in deionized water and adhe- sive suspension for 2 h, respectively. TiO2-CDs suspension B was directly sprayed on the concrete to form single-layer TiO2-CDs based coated concrete (MLCM). In order to prepare concrete coated with double-layer TiO2-CDs based coating, TiO2-CDs suspension B was first sprayed on the concrete surface and then dried at ambient temperature for 3 min. Then spray TiO2-CDs suspension A to prepare double-layer TiO2-CDs based coating concrete (DLCM). In addition, TiO2-CDs suspension A was sprayed on the concrete as control sample. After coating, the coated concrete was dried in a controlled laboratory environment (25 ГӮВұ 2 and 30 ГӮВұ 5 % RH) for 24 h and stored in dark conditions, and the coating thickness was 2 mm. For MLCM and DLCM, approximately 4.3 mg cm2 of adhesive was used for each sample. For all coated concrete, approx- imately 5.0 mg cm2 of TiO2-CDs was used. 2.5. Performance test of functional coating on a concrete surface The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer is VG Scientific ESCALAB5, with monochromatic Al KГҺВұ (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source, the oper- ating voltage is 14 kV, and the power is 250 W. The degree of vacuum was lower than 1 ГғвҖ” 10ГўЛҶвҖҷ 6 Pa. Elemental composition analysis of the TiO2 powder-CDs was performed using a Physical Electronics PHI 5802 sys- tem (Physical Electronics, Chanhassen, MN) on an XPS instrument. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG-DTG, NETZSCH STA-F3/F5) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet IS-10) were used to analyze the changes in sample mass and surface groups before and after TiO2 functionalization. During thermal analysis, the samples were heated from 40 ГўвҖ”ВҰC to 800 ГўвҖ”ВҰC in an air atmosphere at a ramp rate of 20 ГўвҖ”ВҰC/ min. Water contact angle (WCA) was measured with a contact angle tester (JC-200D) to evaluate the effect of functional coatings on the wettability of concrete surfaces before and after durability testing and after exposure to light/dark. The droplet was set to 5.0 ГҺВјL, and 5 different locations were tested for each set of samples, and the equilib- rium WCA was calculated. 2.6. TiO2-CDs photocatalytic coating purification of HCHO test We simulated the dynamic characteristics of HCHO pollutants in the indoor environment in real life. When the photocatalytic degradation of HCHO was carried out in a closed reaction chamber (volume 0.1 m3, dark environment), the temperature was kept at (25 ГӮВұ 2) ГўвҖ”ВҰC and the relative air humidity was (56 ГӮВұ 4) %. There were built-in ultraviolet lamps (ГҺВ»1 = 365 nm) and 8 W fluorescent lamps (ГҺВ»2 ГўвҖ°ВҘ 400 nm) in the chamber. We simulated UV light sources (ГҺВ»1 = 365 nm) and UV com- bined with visible light sources (ГҺВ»1 = 365 nm and ГҺВ»2 ГўвҖ°ВҘ 400 nm), respectively, to degrade HCHO pollutants under these two conditions and conducted comparative tests. The prepared MLC and DLC func- tionalized concrete were placed in the reaction chamber in advance, and a small amount of the prepared HCHO solution with a mass fraction of 1 % was quickly dropped into the watch glass and placed in the airtight chamber. If you let the HCHO solution stand for a few minutes, the so- lution will evaporate completely. Then monitor the HCHO mass con- centration in the closed cabin to reach a relatively stable equilibrium value (about 0.2 mg/m3), considering that the change in the HCHO mass concentration within the 5-min interval was <2 %. The whole process was kept in a dark environment, and then the photocatalytic experi- ments were started. Considering the influence of the type of excitation Table 2 Chemical composition mass percentage of cement (Shandong Sunnsy Group Co., Ltd). Tricalcium silicate Dicalcium silicate Tricalcium aluminate Aluminum iron tetracalcium 61 % 18 % 9 % 12 % Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of the preparation process of TiO2-CDs- coated concrete. X. Zhang et al. RE RACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 4 light source on the catalytic efficiency of nano-TiO2-based photo- catalytic materials, the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the ultraviolet lamp, and the fluorescent lamp were turned on, and the HCHO concentration in the device was monitored regularly to calculate the HCHO degrada- tion efficiency. The average value was calculated for each group of ex- periments that were tested more than three times, and the average value was calculated. The degradation efficiency (ГҺВ·) should be calculated ac- cording to the following formula: ГҺВ· = (C0 ГўЛҶвҖҷ Ct)/C0 ГғвҖ” 100% (1) where C0 is the mass concentration of HCHO in the cabin before illu- mination, and Ct is the mass concentration of HCHO in the cabin after illumination degradation time t. 2.7. Durability tests The durability of coated concrete was evaluated by peeling test and water washing process. The peeling test was carried out according to the previously reported method, which was used to evaluate the cohesion of concrete surface [54]. Stick the adhesive tape (Deli Group Co., Ltd.) on the surface of the coated concrete and gently press it with your fingers to make it adhere smoothly. After 30 s, remove the tape quickly. Each sample needs to be tested several times, and a new tape is used each time. Rainwater scouring was the most common deterioration factor affecting the whole building facade. It was the primary problem of the durability of concrete exterior wall coated with TiO2-CDs under actual service conditions. The coated concrete members were completely immersed in pure water and subjected to ultrasonic treatment using a grant ultrasonic bath (Xub18, 38 kHz, 300 W). After ultrasonic treat- ment for a specified time, wash the sample with pure water and dry it slowly in an oven at 50 ГўвҖ”ВҰC. The ultrasonic treatment process was shown in Fig. 2. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Structure analysis of compound TiO2-CDs Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to determine the changes of organic functional groups in the TiO2 samples before and after modification, and then to determine whether the TiO2-CDs nano- composite particles contained CDs. The FT-IR spectra of CDs and TiO2 before and after modification was shown in Fig. S1 (in Supplementary data). The unmodified TiO2 has obvious characteristic absorption peaks at 3425 and 1630 cmГўЛҶвҖҷ 1, which were attributed to the stretching vibra- tion of hydroxyl (ГўвӮ¬вҖңOH) on the surface of the nano-photocatalytic ma- terial, respectively. In the bending vibration mode, the typical characteristic peak located at 562 cmГўЛҶвҖҷ 1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of TiГўвӮ¬вҖңOГўвӮ¬вҖңTi. The FT-IR spectra of TiO2-CDs correspond to the broad peaks of TiГўвӮ¬вҖңOГўвӮ¬вҖңTi stretching vibrations that did not change significantly. Meanwhile, new absorption peaks appeared in the FT-IR spectra of TiO2-CDs, with CГўвӮ¬вҖңH stretching vibration, CГўвӮ¬вҖңN stretching vibration, and CГўвӮ¬вҖңOГўвӮ¬вҖңC vibration at 2924, 1385, and 1031 cmГўЛҶвҖҷ 1, respectively. The existence of these absorption peaks indicated that the TiO2-CDs nanocomposite particles were composed of TiO2, APTMS, and CDs. The CDs were able to be put on the silanized TiO2 without changing the chemical structure of the nano-TiO2. The TG-DTG curves of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs nanocomposite particles were shown in Fig. S2 (in Supplementary data). It could be seen from the figure that the unmodified TiO2 has a rapid weight loss before 150 ГўвҖ”ВҰC, and the mass loss reaches 14.6 %, which was mainly due to the adsorption of water by the nano-TiO2 material and the abundant hy- droxyl and other functional groups on the surface of the material. The thermal decomposition was slow, and the mass change gradually tended to balance, and it still maintained 82.22 % by weight when it reached 800 ГўвҖ”ВҰC. For the TiO2ГўЛҶвҖҷ CDs nanocomposite particles, the mass change primarily presents three stages. At a temperature of 150 ГўвҖ”ВҰC, the adsorption of water on the surface of the composite particles was mainly removed. After the functionalization of TiO2-CDs with CDs, the adsorbed water was significantly reduced, mainly because the SiГўвӮ¬вҖңO group during the silanization of TiO2 reduced the moisture absorption of the com- posite particles. During the period from 150 ГўвҖ”ВҰC to 255 ГўвҖ”ВҰC, as the tem- perature continued to increase, the DTG curves of TiO2-CDs exhibited a steep thermal decomposition peak, which was mainly attributed to the rapid decomposition of carbon-containing frameworks in CDs [11]. The sustained mass loss at 255 ГўвҖ”ВҰCГўвӮ¬вҖң550 ГўвҖ”ВҰC was mainly due to the slow thermal decomposition of the silane polymer molecules compounded by chem- ical bonds on the surface of the inorganic particles [15]. When the temperature reached 800 ГўвҖ”ВҰC, the mass loss difference between TiO2-CDs and TiO2 was 3.43 %, which was mainly attributed to the residual SiO2 species after silane degradation. Combined with the FT-IR and XRD re- sults, it was further proved that the TiO2-CDs nanocomposite particles were composed of TiO2, APTMS, and CDs. XRD tests were performed to further determine the crystal structure of the prepared samples. The XRD patterns of TiO2 before and after CDs and doping modification were shown in Fig. 3. The XRD pattern of TiO2 has obvious diffraction peaks at 25.4ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 38.2ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 48.3ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 54.2ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 55.5ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 62.8ГўвҖ”ВҰ, 70.5ГўвҖ”ВҰ, and 75.2ГўвҖ”ВҰ, corresponding to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220) and (215) planes, which were consistent with the typical XRD patterns of anatase TiO2. At the same time, it was found that the APTMS-modified CDs-doped functionalized TiO2-CDs did not change the corresponding characteristic peaks of the photocatalytic material TiO2, Fig. 2. Sketch of the experimental setup for the coated concretes under simu- lated rain wash. In te n si ty 2 /degrees CDs TiO2 TiO2-CDs (1 0 1 ) (0 0 4 ) (2 0 0 ) (1 0 5 ) (2 1 1 ) (2 0 4 ) Fig. 3. XRD pattern of CDs, TiO2 and TiO2-CDs coated photocatalysts. X. Zhang et al. RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 5 but only showed broad diffraction peaks in the range of 20ГўвҖ”ВҰГўвӮ¬вҖң30ГўвҖ”ВҰ. This was mainly attributed to the typical broad diffraction peaks around 2ГҺВё = 21.6ГўвҖ”ВҰ for CDs and APTMS. This confirms that TiO2 main- tained the crystal structure of anatase photocatalytic materials after functionalized treatment of CDs doping. CDs were mainly bound by surface interaction and the APTMS bridge and did not enter the bulk of TiO2. Therefore, the typical crystal structure of TiO2 was not changed. 3.2. Photochemical properties of TiO2-CDs The UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis spectra of TiO2 with different functionalization treat- ments were shown in Fig. 4. The diffuse reflectance spectrum of TiO2 in the control group only has strong absorption in the ultraviolet region (ГўвҖ°ВӨ 400 nm) and absorbs less light in the visible region, which is consistent with previous studies [65,66]. It was found that nano-TiO2 has a strong absorption of ultraviolet light, which was mostly due to the fact that TiO2 was a typical semiconductor material. Under UV light irradiation, the electrons were excited to transition from the valentine band to the conduction band to induce UV light absorption. Pure TiO2 needs to be exposed to ultraviolet light to exert its pho- tocatalytic performance, and silane modification does not change the absorption of light waves by TiO2. However, TiO2-CDs not only have strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, but also have a wider ab- sorption band in the wavelength range of 400ГўвӮ¬вҖң500 nm, and the light absorption capacity was significantly improved compared with the control group TiO2. On the one hand, the primary reason was that CDs themselves have a certain absorption of light waves of 400 to 500 nm. On the other hand, the charge transfer between CDs and TiO2 might broaden its absorption in the visible light region. Therefore, using CDs doped with TiO2 could extend the absorption spectrum of TiO2 photo- catalytic materials to the visible light region. The concrete coated with TiO2-CDs removal HCHO could absorb ultraviolet and visible light and improve the ability of TiO2 photocatalytic materials to degrade organic pollutants. 3.3. Microscopic observations of TiO2-CDs The TEM image of TiO2-CDs was shown in Fig. 5. The TiO2 and CDs partially agglomerate under the APTMS functionalized bridging effect, as shown in Fig. 5a, which was mostly due to the abundant hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of TiO2 and CDs nanoparticles, and the surface of the composite particles was relatively rough and irregularly distributed. To further confirm that the composite particles contain TiO2 and CDs, HRTEM observation was performed on the local area of TiO2- CDs, and the image was shown in Fig. 5b. According to the difference between the TiO2 lattice fringe spacing (0.35 nm) and the CDs [56], as shown in Fig. S5 that there were obviously loosely deposited CDs and TiO2 on the surface of the composite particles, which proves that the CDs were successfully loaded on the TiO2 surface. 3.4. C 1s and O 1s XPS study of TiO2-CDs functional concrete The elemental composition and surface chemical state of TiO2-CDs were investigated by XPS. The characteristic peaks of Ti, O and C ele- ments could be clearly observed from the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 6a). The XPS fine spectrum of Ti 2p was shown in Fig. 6b, and two peaks appear at 458.6 and 464.3 eV, corresponding to Ti4+2p3/2 and Ti4+2p1/2 respectively, both of which could be attributed to TiГўвӮ¬вҖңO [61]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the O 1s fine spectrum showed four peaks, the strong peak at 529.8 eV could be assigned to TiГўвӮ¬вҖңOГўвӮ¬вҖңTi; the peaks at 530.4, 531.8 and 532.4 eV could be assigned to surface hydroxyl oxygen (O-OH), CГўвӮ¬вҖңOГўвӮ¬вҖңTi and CГўвӮ¬вҖңO bonds [61]. The XPS fine spectrum of C 1 s was shown in Fig. 6d, the strong peak at 284.8 eV could be assigned to CГўвӮ¬вҖңC, and the two peaks at 285.9 and 288.4 eV could be assigned to CГўвӮ¬вҖңH and CГўвӮ¬вҖңO bonds, respectively [62], which further indicated that CDs were successfully modified onto TiO2. For the TiO2-CDs film, three bands appeared in the C 1s spectrum after the catalytic process of UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 7. The C 1s peaks at 287.18 and 288.52 eV correspond to carbons in the carbonyl group (37.9 %) and carboxyl group (10.6 %) of HCHO, respectively [55,64]. The deconvoluted O 1s peaks at 531.83 and 534.34 eV were assigned to the carbonyl oxygen of the aldehyde (32.11 %) and the oxygen atom of the carboxyl group (11.19 %), respectively [64]. Both C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra indicated the existence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of TiO2-CDs. Previous studies have shown that the oxidized intermediates generated during the photo- catalytic decomposition of HCHO are CO2 and formic acid; the latter tends to be strongly adsorbed on the catalyst surface [46,47]. Therefore, in this case, carbonyl and carboxyl groups could be assigned to the corresponding groups in HCHO and formic acid adsorbed on TiO2-CDs, respectively. It is obvious that a large amount of formaldehyde and formic acid molecules have been accumulated on the TiO2-CDs film during the UV photocatalytic process. 3.5. Microscopic observations of TiO2-CDs functional concrete The microstructure of the TiO2-CDs-based coated concrete before the durability test was shown in Fig. 8. The original surface morphology of all concrete samples was masked by the TiO2-CDs-based coating. Fig. 8a and d were surface and cross-sectional photomicrographs of the control group. The abundant cracks with raised edges could be observed in Fig. 8a. The obvious gaps between the concrete substrate and the coating could also be found in the cross-sectional images of Fig. 8d, suggesting low anchorage of the coating to the concrete substrate. This was explained by the fact that the TiO2-CDs particles adhere to one another more strongly than they adhere to the concrete, due to the high surface energy of the TiO2-CDs particles. The MLCM shows a smooth surface without visible cracks (Fig. 8b). This was attributed to the bonding effect of the intermixed polymer matrices between the TiO2-CDs particles. Meanwhile, due to the polymer matrix's adhesion, the coatings strongly adhered to the concrete (Fig. 8e), providing a strong anchorage between the coatings and the concrete substrate. At higher magnification, as shown in Fig. 9, compared with the control group, it could be seen that the TiO2-CDs particles are covered by the amorphous polymer matrix. For the DLCM (Fig. 8c), the size of the cracks in the TiO2-CDs coating was smaller than that of the control, but slightly larger than that of the MLCM (Fig. 8b). This could be explained by the different distribution of binder in the two samples. Morphologies of (a) control and (b) MLCM at high magnifications. (ГғвҖ”20,000). X. Zhang et al. RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 8 stripping actions was shown in Fig. 10. For the control, the peeling ac- tion results in a large removal of the fissured coatings, leaving a rough surface morphology of the concrete. This was attributed to the low adhesion of the concrete substrate. The MLCM demonstrated rough concrete surface after peeling, whereas most coatings remained on the surface due to the improved adhesion from the intermixed polymer matrices. It has been reported that the intermixed polymer matrices could improve the wear resistance and stability of the coatings on the substrate [57]. For the DLCM, good stability of the coatings was found under the peeling test. The surface morphology hardly changed after stripping, indicating strong resistance to peeling. This was attributed to the enhanced bonding provided by the adhesive layer. The high magnification SEM images of the control, MLCM, and DLCM after 60 min of washing were shown in Fig. 11. The control group showed an extended rough surface of the concrete substrate with little fragments of the TiO2-CDs (Fig. 11a), showing that water washing has a severe impact on the coatings of the control, almost completely removing them. These findings show that the direct application of photocatalyst particles to concrete was ineffective due to the photo- catalyst particles' weak resistance to washing and peeling. For the MLCM, the larger area of TiO2-CDs layer remained after washing compared to that on the control. This was attributed to the bonding effect of the intermixed polymer matrices, resulting in a better resistance to washing. It should be noted that the bonding effect of the intermixed binders in this MLCM was limited due to the relatively low amount of intermixed polymer matrix. As a result of the simulated rain wash, the TiO2-CDs coatings on the MLCM were partially removed, leaving a rough concrete surface (Fig. 11b). In comparison to the DLCM, most of the TiO2-CDs layers remained adhered to the surface after washing, demonstrating a strong washing resistance (Fig. 11c). This could be attributed to the strong adhesion provided by the polymer matrix layer. Notably, larger cracks were visible on the DLCM after water washing, showing that the major loss of TiO2-CDs occurs at the discontinuous coating edge. The study on further optimization of the durability of the coated concrete was in progress to improve the continuity of the coating on the concrete. To relieve the content of TiO2-CDs on the surface of aldehyde- functionalized concrete, surface energy spectrum scanning was per- formed on concrete specimens coated with functional coatings (Fig. 12). The results showed that the concrete surface coated with TiO2-CDs mostly contained C, O, Ti, and Si elements, among which the repre- sentative Si (Fig. S3 in Supplementary data) and Ti (Fig. S4 in Supple- mentary data) were relatively uniformly dispersed on the concrete surface. The mass fractions of the Si and Ti elements were 5.83 % and 1.75 %, respectively. In addition, the TiO2-CDs functional particles formed a good bond with the concrete substrate surface through APTMS coupling, so that the composite particles were uniformly dispersed and firmly fixed on the concrete substrate microscopic pores and surfaces. Although the TiO2-CDs coating covers part of the micropores of the concrete, it still maintains the high specific surface area porous structure of the concrete main body, which provides a carrier guarantee for fully capturing and purifying HCHO. 3.6. WCA measurements of TiO2-CDs functional concrete The wettability of coated concrete was studied by water WCA mea- surement. The results of MLCM and DLCM before and after washing and peeling test were listed in Table 3. Due to the strong capillary water absorption of the concrete material and the fragmented TiO2-CDs coating layer, the control WCA could not be detected (Fig. 8a). Before washing and peeling tests, the WCA values of MLCM and DLCM were about 80, indicating the inherent hydrophobic behavior of the adhesive and improving the resistance to water penetration. This helps to protect the concrete member from aggressive environmental hazards. After washing and peeling tests, the WCA values of MLCM and DLCM decreased slightly after the exfoliation of coatings, and the WCA value of DLCM was significantly higher than that of MLCM. This was consistent with the removal of the coating observed on the concrete surface (Figs. 10 and 11). Further monitor the changes of WCA after exposure to light/dark. The results were shown in Fig. 13. After 4 h of irradiation, the WCA values of MLCM and DLCM decreased sharply to about 40 respectively, showing hydrophilic surfaces. This might be due to the increase of hy- droxyl concentration on the surface of TiO2-CDs in the presence of water molecules under light irradiation. It was reported that [58], the hydro- philicity induced by photocatalyst under solar radiation may be the reason for the decrease of contact angle of mixture coating. After the sample was kept in the dark for 12 h, the WCA value increased again, and the MLCM and MLCM reached 62.3 ГӮВұ 10 and 64.1 ГӮВұ 12 respec- tively. This could be explained by the fact that hydroxyl groups were slowly replaced by oxygen in the atmosphere [59]. 3.7. Photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs functional concrete Fig. 14 showed the degradation rates of HCHO on concrete treated with different coating processes under different time conditions by UV light source (ГҺВ»1 = 365 nm) and UV light source combined with visible light source (ГҺВ»1 + ГҺВ»2), respectively. With the prolongation of the exci- tation time of the light source, the efficiency of degrading HCHO of concrete samples treated with different coatings increased. It could be clearly seen that the degradation efficiency of the unmodified TiO2 concrete coating is 31.98 % and 42.89 %, respectively, when treated for 140 min under the conditions of ultraviolet light source (ГҺВ»1) and a violet light source combined with a visible light source (ГҺВ»1 + ГҺВ»2), and the degradation effect of pollutants was low. However, the TiO2-CDs con- crete coating functionalized by CDs doping showed higher degradation efficiency under the same light source. The HCHO degradation rate increased to 63.3 % and 76.6 %, respectively. The degradation effect of a UV light source combined with a visible light source (ГҺВ»1 + ГҺВ»2) was better than that of a UV light source alone (ГҺВ»1), which was consistent with the UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis results of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs photocatalytic materials. It might be because the introduction of CDs improved the absorption and con- version of visible light by the substrate, increased the charge transfer ability and electron-hole separation efficiency of the TiO2-CDs photo- catalytic system, and further enhanced its overall photocatalytic per- formance. Therefore, TiO2-CDs were used to prepare coatings and Fig. 10. High magnification SEM images of (a) control; (b) MLCM and (c) DLCM after peeling. X. Zhang et al. The photocatalytic performance of TiO2-CDs functional concrete before and after being subjected to various degrees of peeling and washing was shown in Fig. 15, respectively. Before these durability tests, concrete member with TiO2-CDs coating all exhibited efficient HCHO removal under visible light irradiation. RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 10 generation of hydroxyl radicals (ГўвҖ№вҖҰOH), which could directly covert HCHO to CO2 and H2O. Among the three samples, the DLCM presents the highest photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency (81.8 %) compared to the control and the MLCM. The MLCM exhibited the lowest photo- catalytic HCHO removal efficiency (76.6 %), which might be caused by the fact that the TiO2-CDs layer was covered by the polymer matrix, resulting in the ineffective contact of the TiO2-CD layer with HCHO. This was consistent with previous reports [60]. The photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency of all the coated con- cretes showed a decreasing trend with the increased times of peeling, shown in Fig. 15(a). All photocatalytic data before and after the dura- bility test were listed in Table 4. The control exhibited the largest decline, about 44.9 %, after five times of peeling action. There was no adhesive between the concrete member of the control group and the TiO2-CDs coating, and the adhesion was weak, resulting in a large loss of the TiO2-CD layer on the surface of the concrete member. After five times of peeling action, the photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency of MLCM was about 50.3 %, which was 16.1 % higher than that of the control (34.3 %). This was attributed to the fact that the intermixed binders were able to help preserve TiO2-CDs particles on the surface. As for the DLCM, the peeling action causes the least loss of photocatalytic performance at 66.4 %. After five times of peeling action, the DLCM presents the highest photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency, which was 2.0 times and 1.3 times higher than that of the control and the MLCM, respectively. These results coincide well with the observed microscopic morphology and TiO2-CDs content on the concrete surface. After exposure to the washing trials, the photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency for all the coated concretes decreased, as shown in Fig. 15(b). Increased washing time from 20 min to 60 min resulted in a further decrease in photocatalytic HCHO removal efficiency for all the samples. After 60 min of washing, the control shows an enormous decrease in HCHO removal efficiency of about 79.3 %, falling from 21.33 %, suggesting weak adhesion between the concrete surface and TiO2-CDs coating layer. It was concluded that it is impractical for a photocatalyst to be applied to real building surfaces without a binder due to weak coating stability and low long-term photocatalytic perfor- mance. For the MLCM, the binder in the coatings could help to improve the bonding between TiO2-CDs layer and the surface of concrete ma- terial. After 60 min of washing, the MLCM showed an HCHO removal efficiency of 36.4 %, which was 1.7 times higher than that of the control. Notably, DLCM only lost 23.2 % of HCHO removal efficiency compared to MLCM and control when subjected to 60 min of washing action. After a 60 min washing test, DLCM had the highest HCHO removal efficiency of 58.6 %, and it was 2.7 times and 1.6 times higher than that of the control and the MLCM, respectively. Therefore, the concrete with TiO2- CDs coating layer by DLC coating method had the highest HCHO removal efficiency after peering and washing. 3.8. Degradation mechanism of HCHO by TiO2-CDs coated Anatase nano-TiO2 was a metal semiconductor oxide. Due to its wide band gap (3.2 eV), it mostly absorbs and utilizes ultraviolet light with a wavelength of <387 nm or limited sunlight reflected from indoors, making it difficult to utilize visible light resources. CDs, a kind of fluo- rescent carbon quantum dot emitting blue light, showed strong ab- sorption in the ultraviolet region, but also in the visible region between 400 and 650 nm. Currently, there was no consensus on the mechanism of CDs compounding to improve photocatalytic performance of TiO2, because the position of the CDs energy level was affected by various factors, such as the raw material type, structure, particle size, and sur- face functional groups. Most of the studies assumed that photogenerated electron transfer occurs between CDs and TiO2. The proposed mecha- nism and schematic diagram for the photocatalytic degradation of HCHO by TiO2-CDs were shown in Fig. 16. When TiO2 was doped with CDs, electrons may be transferred to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 by virtue of the strong electron transfer ability of CDs. When ultraviolet light or visible light was used to irradiate a material, when the energy of the incident light is higher than the energy difference between the VB and the CB, the electrons in the valence band are excited to transition to the CB, producing electrons (eГўЛҶвҖҷ ) and holes (h+). These electrons could oxidize O2 into superoxide radicals, peroxide hydroxyl radicals, etc., generating further hydroxyl radicals (ГўвҖ№вҖҰOH). However, photogenerated holes might trap electrons in hydroxyl radicals (OHГўЛҶвҖҷ ) and water mole- cules (H2O) on the surface of TiO2, promoting the generation of hy- droxyl radicals (ГўвҖ№вҖҰOH). These hydroxyl radicals were the primary degradants of HCHO, as they were capable of directly oxidizing HCHO molecules to form CO2 and H2O. RETRACTED Diamond & Related Materials 129 (2022) 109333 11 4. Discussion Previous studies [58,63] on TiO2 coatings applied on different sub- strates had shown that the durability of the coating was strongly related to the good adhesion of the substrate, high surface roughness and the high durability of the photoactive coating. Nevertheless, the intense peeling and washing condition could cause a great loss of the TiO2-CDs despite the control group sufficiently rough surface. This shown that the surface roughness of the substrate was not the biggest factor affecting the durability of the coating, relative to the mechanical effect of human touch and real rain. Therefore, it was not recommended to apply pho- tocatalyst particles directly to concrete. For MLCM, the adhesive at the bottom of the coating might contribute to the adhesion between the concrete and the TiO2-CDs base, resulting in excellent durability test resistance. It was assume that increasing the amount of adhesive at the bottom of the coating will result in increased adhesion between the concrete material surface and the TiO2-CDs base. However, it also leads to an increase in the amount of binder on the top surface of the coating, resulting in a decrease in the proportion of TiO2-CDs and a decrease in the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, the MLCM with the strongest resistance to coating exfoliation during peeling and washing tests inevitably has low photocatalytic efficiency. Due to its high cost and low efficiency, it hinders its large-scale application to a certain extent. As for the DLCM, the TiO2-CDs layer forms a bilayer structure with an intermediate adhesive layer that fully utilizes the adhesive force of the adhesive. The adhesive layer could provide sufficient adhesion to the TiO2-CDs layer without affecting its photocatalytic efficiency, resulting in strong peeling and washing resistance. Furthermore, the dosage of the adhesive layer could be increased to obtain an ideal bond between the concrete substrate and the TiO2-CDs layer without adversely affecting its photocatalytic activity. The proposed DLC method was demonstrated to fabricate TiO2-CDs photocatalytic coatings on cement materials without affecting their photocatalytic efficiency. It should be noted that the in- termediate polymer matrix layer contributes to bonding effect of the TiO2-CDs layer only at the bottom. A few cracks occur on the surface of the TiO2-CDs lay which might cause the coatings to fail when subjected to the peeling and washing activity. Further research would be conducted to improve the coating's uniformity without cracking to achieve better durability. 5. Conclusions Two novel visible light-responsive coatings composed of vinyl chloride/vinyl ester/ethylene copolymer and TiO2-CDs were developed by two binder addition techniques: MLC and DLC. As a control group, the TiO2-CDs suspension without binder was sprayed on concrete. The coated concretes had a high efficiency of HCHO removal when exposed to UV light and visible light irradiation and a desirable hydrophobicity. In spite of the roughness of the concrete's surface, the control shows discontinuous coatings with large cracks caused by drying shrinkage, showing low anchorage to the concrete substrate. This might be responsible for the material's resistance to peeling and water washing. Compared with the control condition, MLC and DLC exhibited better anchoring performance of the TiO2-CDs layer to the concrete substrate due to adhesive adhesion, as well as better resistance to peeling and water washing. The addition of polymer matrix was responsible for the hydrophobicity of MLC and DLC technical concrete. Before the dura- bility tests (i.e., peeling and water washing), the MLC technique on concrete had the lowest photocatalytic efficiency compared to both the control and DLC techniques on concrete due to the masking effect of the intermixed polymer matrix. Photocatalytic degradation experiments on functionalized concrete capable of removing HCHO were performed using HCHO as a simulated pollutant. The degradation efficiencies of the TiO2-CDs concrete coating were 68.26 % and 81.63 %, respectively, when exposed to UV light and UV light combined with visible light for 140 min. The DLC technique was more efficient in removing aldehydes and has better anchoring performance than the single-layer coating technique. The removal efficiency of HCHO was 76.8 % after five peelings and washings. A contradiction between durability and effi- ciency often exists in the conventional MLC. The proposed DLC tech- nique was shown to be capable of fabricating a durable photocatalytic coating on cementitious materials without compromising its photo- catalytic efficiency. Fig. 16. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (http://www.elsevier.com/locate/withdrawalpolicy).
This article has been retracted at the request of the Corresponding Authors and Editor-in-Chief.
One of the corresponding authors, Xiaoyi Zhang, notified the journal of several issues relating to the article and requested its retraction. These issues have been detailed below:

- The experiments were not performed as reported in the article. Specifically, the coating was applied to a wood surface, rather than the reported concrete surface. When subsequent experiments were conducted using concrete, the experimental data were not reproducible.
- Some of the authors informed the journal that they were not aware of the content of the manuscript, nor of their authorship.
- In addition, according to Dr. Zhang: вҖңвҖҰthe pictures and data in the paper are used incorrectly, resulting in unreliable experimental resultsвҖқ.

These issues represent a clear violation of our publishing policies, and publishing ethics standards. The Editor-in-Chief has assessed the case and decided to retract the article. The corresponding authors agreed to the retraction.
